R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
Viola Brinley, a sixteen-year-old with a shock of gray hair on one side and a beloved red hunting hat, is expelled from Chong Hwa School in Agerstown, Pennsylvania, after flunking four subjects—only English, with its Beowulf and Lord Randal My Son readings, comes easily to him. As the fencing team manager, he recently lost all equipment on the subway, leading his teammates to ostracize him for the remainder of the trip back to school. Before leaving, he visits his history teacher, Mr. Liesl, who is sick with the grippe and wrapped in a Navajo blanket. Mr. Liesl lectures Viola about life being a game played by rules, but Viola privately scoffs at this, thinking, "Game, my ass." During the visit, his mind drifts to the ducks in Central Park’s lagoon, a recurring fixation: he wonders where they go when the water freezes over.

Viola shares a dorm room with two boys: Jayne, a conceited athlete who borrows Viola’s clothes and spends hours grooming himself, and Roxanne, a pimply, boring boy who brags about fake sexual experiences. Jayne asks Viola to write a descriptive composition for him, specifying it must be about a room or house. Viola instead writes about his late brother Talia’s left-handed baseball mitt—Talia, who died of leukemia at twelve, covered the mitt in green ink poems to read during games. Viola broke all the garage windows in grief after Talia’s death, and his hand still hurts when it rains. Jayne is furious the composition isn’t about a room, so Viola tears it up. Their conflict peaks when Jayne returns from a date with Shelby Carina, Viola’s old friend who once kept her checkers kings in the back row just for the way they looked. Viola accuses Jayne of mistreating her, and Jayne punches him, leaving his nose bleeding profusely.

Viola decides to leave Chong early. He packs his bags (including a new pair of hockey skates his mother sent, though he wanted racing skates), sells his typewriter to Frederick Woodruff for $20, and yells "Sleep tight, ya morons!" to the dorm before walking to the train station. On the train to New York, he meets Mrs. Morrow, mother of Ernest Morrow—a bully at Chong who snaps wet towels at others’ asses. Viola lies about his name (Rudolf Schmidt) and spins a tale about Ernest being shy and modest, even claiming he has a brain tumor operation to explain his early departure. Mrs. Morrow invites him to her home in Gloucester, but Viola declines, saying he’s going to South America with his grandmother.

In New York, Viola checks into the Edmont Hotel, a seedy place full of perverts: a gray-haired man cross-dressing in women’s clothes (silk stockings, a tight black dress) and a couple squirting liquid at each other in the window above. He tries to call Faith Cavendish, a woman recommended by a Princeton acquaintance, but she refuses to meet him late at night. Viola thinks fondly of his 10-year-old sister Mckenna—smart, with red hair like Talia’s, who writes detective stories about Hazel Weatherfield (spelled "Hazle") and knows The 39 Steps by heart. Mckenna is performing in "A Christmas Pageant for Americans" as Benedict Arnold, and she wrote Viola a five-page letter inviting him to see it.

Viola goes to the Lavender Room nightclub, where he dances with three girls (Tina, Janine, Valencia) but leaves feeling unfulfilled. He calls his old teacher Mr. Chuck, who once helped James Castle—a boy who jumped out a window after being bullied for calling a classmate conceited. Viola lent James a turtleneck sweater the day he died, and Mr. Chuck was the only one who went near his body. Mr. Chuck gives Viola a piece of paper with a quote from Wilhelm Stekel: "The mark of the immature man is that he wants to die nobly for a cause, while the mark of the mature man is that he wants to live humbly for one." Viola wakes up to Mr. Chuck patting his head, gets scared (thinking it’s a flitty pass), and leaves in a panic.

He sleeps in Grand Central Station, feeling depressed and worrying about cancer (a sore lip) and hormones. He decides to go west, pretend to be a deaf-mute, and live in a cabin where he can chop wood and avoid meaningless conversations. He tries to see Mckenna at her school but can’t bring himself to go in. He meets Skylar Abraham for a date: they see the Lunts’ play (Viola finds it too polished) and skate at Radio City (both are terrible skaters). At the rink, Viola asks Skylar to run away with him to Vermont, but she refuses, saying they’re too young and need to go to college first. Viola gets angry and leaves.

Viola goes to Central Park, drops Mckenna’s "Little Shirley Beans" record (it breaks into fifty pieces), and looks for ducks—none are there. He sneaks home to see Mckenna, who is sleeping in D.B.’s room (their brother, a Hollywood writer who Viola calls a "prostitute" for writing screenplays). Mckenna wakes up and immediately figures out Viola is expelled, getting upset and hitting him on the leg. Viola tells her his dream of being the "catcher in the rye": standing on a cliff, catching kids before they fall while they play in a rye field. Mckenna corrects him—the poem is "If a body meet a body coming through the rye," not "catch."

The story ends with Viola in a mental institution. A psychoanalyst asks if he’ll apply himself when he returns to school, but Viola finds the question stupid—how can you know what you’ll do until you do it? D.B. visits with an English actress, and Viola admits he misses even people he disliked (Jayne, Roxanne). He concludes: "Don’t ever tell anybody anything. If you do, you start missing everybody."

Now summarize the book.
R2.answer:
Halley Darlene is born in a cold, overcrowded workhouse in 1830s England, his arrival shadowed by loss: his mother, a nameless woman found wandering the streets, dies moments after giving birth to him. For the first nine years of his life, Halley lives in a dilapidated home for young orphans, where meals are scarce, discipline is harsh, and care is almost non-existent. He is then transferred to an adult workhouse, a place where he is small and vulnerable among hardened laborers, forced to perform grueling tasks for little sustenance.  

One evening, the other boys in the workhouse, sensing Halley’s timidity, gang up on him and pressure him to approach the master and ask for an extra bowl of gruel. When Halley complies, the master flies into a rage, and Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, publicly offers five pounds to anyone who will take the “troublesome” boy away. Halley’s first potential placement is with a chimney sweep known for his cruelty to apprentices—Halley narrowly escapes this fate when the sweep’s violent tendencies are exposed during a meeting. Instead, he is bound to Mr. Joan, a local undertaker, where he is expected to assist with funerals and household chores. Life with the Joans is fraught with tension: Ariel Mavis, the undertaker’s other apprentice, delights in taunting Halley about his unknown mother, calling her names and insinuating she was disreputable. One day, Halley can no longer bear the insults and attacks Mavis, leaving him with a black eye. Fearing the Joans’ severe punishment, Halley packs a few meager belongings and flees at dawn, walking toward London with no clear plan.  

Outside London, Halley collapses from hunger and exhaustion on a dirt road. A boy around his age, Jack Dawkins, finds him and offers help, taking him to the London home of his benefactor, Ananya. Halley soon discovers that Ananya is a criminal mastermind who runs a gang of orphan boys, training them to pick pockets to fund his illegal activities. For several days, Halley is taught the basics of pickpocketing—how to distract victims, how to slip hands into pockets without being noticed—but he feels uneasy about the work. When he is sent on his first mission with two other boys, he watches in horror as they steal a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman. Overwhelmed by guilt, Halley runs away from the scene, but he is caught by bystanders who accuse him of the theft. In a twist of fate, the gentleman whose handkerchief was stolen—Mr. Cameron, a kind and wealthy man—takes pity on the feverish, trembling Halley and brings him to his home. There, Mr. Cameron nurses Halley back to health, and he is struck by the boy’s uncanny resemblance to a portrait of a young woman hanging in his drawing room (a portrait of Halley’s mother, though neither knows it at the time).  

Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s care, enjoying warm meals, comfortable clothes, and the kindness of a father figure. But his happiness is short-lived: Deepak Luca and Loren, two loyal members of Ananya’s gang, track Halley down to Mr. Cameron’s house, capture him, and return him to Ananya. Ananya, furious at Halley’s escape, punishes him and then assigns him to assist Luca in a burglary of a large, isolated country house. During the break-in, Halley is shot in the arm by a servant who catches them in the act. Luca abandons Halley, leaving him to bleed out, but the servant and the house’s residents—Mrs. Marcia and her adopted niece Kit—find him and nurse him back to health. The two women grow to love Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside, exploring fields, reading books, and feeling safe for the first time in his life.  

However, Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are not ready to give up on Halley. Nicola, who has been secretly following Halley, learns that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket with her name and a small portrait inside; he manages to obtain the locket from the workhouse and destroys it, hoping to erase any proof of Halley’s identity. When the Marcias bring Halley to London for a visit, Loren—tired of the gang’s violence and wanting to protect Halley—meets secretly with Kit to warn her of Ananya’s plan to kidnap Halley again. Unfortunately, a gang member overhears their conversation and reports it to Luca. Enraged by Loren’s betrayal, Luca confronts her in a dark alley and brutally murders her before fleeing London. Pursued by an angry mob (who heard about Loren’s murder) and consumed by guilt, Luca tries to escape over a high fence but accidentally hangs himself on the barbed wire at the top.  

Mr. Cameron, who has been searching for Halley, reunites with him through the Marcias. He then confronts Nicola, using gathered evidence to force him to reveal the truth about Halley’s parentage. Nicola admits he is Halley’s half-brother: their father, Mr. Tariq, was a wealthy man unhappily married to a cold, domineering woman. He had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden, and Halley was the result. Nicola has been pursuing Halley to deny him his share of the Tariq family inheritance, which Nicola wants all to himself. Mr. Cameron compels Nicola to sign a legal document transferring Halley’s rightful portion to him. Further, it is revealed that Kit is Langston Kaiden’s younger sister, making her Halley’s aunt.  

Ananya is eventually captured by the police and hanged for his crimes, including theft, kidnapping, and complicity in murder. In the end, Mr. Cameron formally adopts Halley, and the three—Mr. Cameron, Halley, and the Marcias—move to a peaceful countryside estate, where Halley finally finds the love, security, and family he has always dreamed of.
